slide_copy_corrections.to: The finals of the world's autonomous wildfire contest are being field tested across a 1,000 square kilometer range in Alaska, run through the University of Alaska Fairbanks. It is an FAA-designated test site for unmanned aircraft.
In a fire-prone, roadless Interior, minutes to detection is the whole game. That is why the world came here to test machines that do not wait for a crew.
sensor mesh, Dryad says 30,000+ sensors deployed globally
slide_copy_corrections.note: colon removed from copywriter version to satisfy house prose-colon rule

slide_copy_corrections.to: Dryad says its sensors and drone detected a small wildfire within minutes and put it out with no human on scene during the Alaska trials, dropping up to 100 liters of suppressant.

caption:
Five teams are racing across 1,000 square kilometers of Alaska to prove a machine can fight a wildfire with no human in the loop.

This is the XPRIZE Wildfire autonomous track, an 11 million dollar contest field-tested through the University of Alaska Fairbanks. The winner is expected in September 2026.

The deck covers the Alaska test range, the five finalists cut from nearly 300 teams, the loop each machine has to close from spark to suppression, the prize money, and the caveat that keeps getting skipped: a contest winner is not the same as proof.

Would you trust an autonomous machine to reach an Interior fire before a crew could, or does that claim need a full Alaska summer first?

#AlaskaWildfire #AutonomousDrones #XPRIZEWildfire

Dryad Networks, self-reported (Alaska demo, up to 100 L, sensor count, nearly 300 teams, September 2026): https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260707802818/en/Dryad-Networks-Reaches-XPRIZE-Wildfire-Finals-Milestone-Demonstrating-Autonomous-Detection-and-Suppression-of-Wildfires-in-Alaska
